units.lore: The Humber IV was the upgunned variant carrying the American 37mm M6 gun in a redesigned turret, giving the divisional cavalry regiment an armoured car with genuine anti-armour punch against the light armour and armoured cars it was likely to encounter on patrol. The 37mm gave it parity with the German Sd Kfz 222 and superiority over Italian armoured cars, making it a capable fighting vehicle for the aggressive patrolling style of British divisional reconnaissance. The Humber IV served extensively in Italy and NW Europe, often operating as troop leaders' vehicles within mixed reconnaissance troops.
units.name: Humber IV

units.lore: The AEC Armoured Car was designed and built without official requirement by the Associated Equipment Company as a private venture, resulting in the heaviest British wheeled armoured car of the war. The Mark I carried the 2-pounder in a Valentine turret on a heavy bus-derived chassis, giving it armor protection that exceeded most conventional armoured cars but at the cost of poor cross-country performance and a stiff ride. It served with the 11th Hussars and other armoured car regiments in North Africa, where its heavy armour was appreciated in the long-range desert patrols where a breakdown hundreds of miles from support could be fatal.
units.name: AEC I

units.lore: The AEC Mark III was the final and most powerful variant, replacing the 2-pounder and 6-pounder of earlier marks with the American 75mm M3 gun — the same weapon carried in the Sherman — giving it an unprecedented combination of anti-armour firepower and armoured protection in a wheeled vehicle. With the 75mm it could engage Panzer IVs on even terms and threaten Panthers at short range, making it a capable fighting vehicle rather than merely a reconnaissance asset. AEC III cars served in Italian and NW European armoured car regiments, often leading actions against German rear-area defences where their heavy armament was decisive.
units.name: AEC III

units.lore: The Universal Carrier — universally called the Bren Gun Carrier despite carrying far more than just Brens — was the most produced British armoured fighting vehicle of the war with over 113,000 built across the Commonwealth. Open-topped and lightly armoured, it gave infantry platoons and support weapons a cross-country mobility asset that could move a crew-served weapon, carry ammunition, or serve as a command vehicle in the battalion commander's tactical group. Despite its limitations — it was not a fighting vehicle in any serious sense — the Universal Carrier was the logistical backbone of the British infantry at platoon and company level in every theater.
units.name: Universal Carrier

units.lore: The Boys anti-tank rifle-armed Universal Carrier was the standard British reconnaissance troop vehicle in 1939-42, before the Boys' marginal penetration against German armour made it obsolete in the anti-tank role. The Boys rifle's 0.55-inch round could penetrate 20mm of armour at 100 meters, which was adequate against the Panzer I, II, and most armoured cars encountered in the early war campaigns. Carrier patrols typically operated in pairs, using speed and cover to observe German positions and withdraw before heavier German vehicles could engage them, the Boys rifle providing enough deterrence against opposing light forces to give the crews confidence to operate independently.
units.name: Universal Carrier (Boys)

units.lore: The PIAT-armed Universal Carrier gave British and Commonwealth carrier platoons a vehicle-mounted anti-tank capability from 1943 onward, replacing the obsolete Boys rifle with a weapon that could genuinely threaten German medium tanks at close range. The PIAT's spring-operated mechanism was difficult to cock inside the cramped carrier, so crews typically dismounted to engage armour — the carrier providing rapid mobility to a firing position and quick extraction afterward. These vehicles screened infantry advances, patrolled between defended localities, and provided the close anti-armour protection that battalion commanders needed to deter opportunistic German tank raids against their perimeters.
units.name: Universal Carrier (PIAT)

units.lore: Mounting the .50-calibre Browning on the Universal Carrier gave reconnaissance units a vehicle-mounted heavy machine gun for both anti-aircraft and anti-personnel roles. The Browning's flat trajectory and heavy round could engage soft-skin vehicles, gun crews, and low-flying aircraft with equal effectiveness, making these carriers valuable in the advance to contact when air threat and road ambush were constant concerns. Canadian and British reconnaissance regiments employed these as troop fire-support vehicles, providing the suppressive fire while lighter Dingo scout cars pushed forward to observe.
units.name: Universal Carrier (.5" MG)

units.lore: The Indian Pattern Carrier was a simplified Universal Carrier variant produced in Australia and India for the Pacific and Mediterranean theaters, with modifications suited to tropical operations and Commonwealth manufacturing capabilities. Used extensively by Indian Army divisions in Burma, it carried patrol teams through jungle terrain that wheeled vehicles could not negotiate, often serving as the only mechanical transport available to forward battalions operating on jungle tracks. In the Mediterranean, it equipped the carrier platoons of Indian divisions throughout the Italian campaign, providing the same cross-country reconnaissance and weapons-movement capability as the standard Universal Carrier.
units.name: Indian Pattern Carrier

units.lore: The Daimler Dingo scout car was arguably the finest small reconnaissance vehicle of the war — fast, reliable, and so nimble that experienced crews could navigate terrain that frustrated larger vehicles. Its fluid flywheel transmission allowed smooth speed changes in tight terrain, and the small, flat profile made it genuinely difficult to spot in hedgerows and olive groves. The Dingo's role was pure intelligence-gathering: get forward, observe, and report without being seen. It was normally paired with a Daimler armoured car in reconnaissance troops, the Dingo pushing furthest forward while the armoured car provided covering fire from a position to the rear.
units.name: Daimler Dingo

units.lore: The Humber Light Reconnaissance Car was a wheeled, lightly armoured vehicle designed for the divisional reconnaissance regiment's close-patrol work — finding the enemy's outpost line and reporting without being drawn into combat. Its Boys anti-tank rifle provided minimal anti-armour capability mainly as a deterrent against other light vehicles, while the rear-mounted AA machine gun gave some air-defence protection during road movement. Humber LRCs typically operated in troops of three, with two cars observing while one maintained communication with higher headquarters, leapfrogging forward in bounds as the advance progressed.
units.name: Humber LRC III

units.lore: The Otter was a Canadian-built version of the Humber LRC, produced at the General Motors of Canada plant to meet Commonwealth reconnaissance demands that British factories couldn't fill. Essentially identical in capability and role to the Humber, it served primarily with Canadian reconnaissance regiments in Italy and NW Europe. The Boys anti-tank rifle was its nominal main armament, though by 1943 most crew commanders knew it was nearly useless against German armour and used the vehicle as an observation platform that could survive near misses and reach forward ground quickly rather than as a fighting vehicle.
units.name: Otter LRC I

units.lore: The self-propelled Bofors 40mm mounted on various wheeled or half-tracked vehicles gave British light AA regiments the ability to accompany armoured columns and provide air-defence coverage in the advance rather than remaining static at airfields and supply points. As the Luftwaffe's ground-attack capability declined from 1944, these vehicles increasingly turned their fire against ground targets — the Bofors's 2-pound shell was highly effective against light armour, soft vehicles, and infantry in the open. Commonwealth armoured car regiments sometimes used SP Bofors as close-support vehicles for their wheeled cars, the gun's flat trajectory and high rate of fire providing devastating suppressive fire.
units.name: Bofors 40mm SP

units.lore: The Canadian Military Pattern (CMP) truck and its British equivalents — the Bedford QL, Morris Commercial, and Austin K-series — formed the logistical backbone of the British and Commonwealth armies. Standardized across all Commonwealth manufacturers to common specifications, they provided the vehicle pool that moved every artillery round, ration pack, and fuel drum from beach or railhead to battalion forward supply point. Motor battalions in armoured divisions rode to battle in these trucks, dismounting to fight while the drivers pulled back to a covered position; their loss to air attack, ambush, or breakdown directly constrained how far and how fast armies could operate.
units.name: CMP/Bedford/Morris Truck

units.lore: The Morris C8 Field Artillery Tractor — universally called the "Quad" — was the standard prime mover for British field and anti-tank guns, designed to tow the 25-pounder and its limber or a 6-pounder anti-tank gun with its crew riding in the enclosed cab and rear ammunition compartment. Its four-wheel drive and heavy-duty suspension allowed it to pull a loaded 25-pounder across fields and through muddy tracks that would stop a wheeled truck, while the enclosed body gave the gun crew some protection against shell fragments and weather during the move to the gun line. The Quad and its gun were an inseparable combination in Commonwealth artillery units throughout the war.
units.name: Morris C8/Quad/AT Tractor

units.lore: The White Scout Car and its Marmon-Herrington equivalent were lightly armoured, open-topped liaison and command vehicles used throughout the Middle East and Mediterranean campaigns by Commonwealth forces. Their high ground clearance and four-wheel drive made them useful in desert and mountain terrain, and they served as forward headquarters vehicles, liaison officer transports, and mobile command posts. Commonwealth units in North Africa used them extensively in the long-range patrol work of the Western Desert Force, operating hundreds of miles from base with minimal support in a theatre where vehicle reliability and range were as critical as armour and firepower.
units.name: White/Marmon Scout Car

units.lore: The Humber Scout Car was a small, lightly armoured liaison vehicle used by British divisional headquarters for courier duties, forward observation officer transport, and command-element mobility in the NW Europe campaign. Essentially an armoured version of a light car, it gave officers moving between headquarters and forward battalions a degree of protection against shell fragments and small-arms fire without the bulk or noise of a Universal Carrier. Its single AA machine gun provided some defensive capability, and the enclosed hull protected occupants against weather during the wet autumn and winter campaigns in the Low Countries.
units.name: Humber Scout Car

units.lore: The M5 Half-Track was the International Harvester-produced version of the standard American M3, supplied to Britain under Lend-Lease to equip the motor platoons of armoured divisions that needed infantry transport with cross-country capability. British motor battalions in armoured divisions rode these halftracks in the advance to contact, dismounting to clear villages and hold ground while the tanks pressed on. Unlike American armored infantry who sometimes fought from their halftracks, British doctrine generally had infantry dismount before the final assault, using the halftrack primarily for protected movement rather than as a fighting vehicle.
units.name: M5 Half-Track

units.lore: Soviet rifle sections combined Mosin-Nagant riflemen with the Degtyaryov DP light machine gun — a reliable pan-magazine LMG that gave each squad automatic fire from 1941 onward. Early war infantry suffered catastrophic losses to German encirclements and superior tactics, but survivors became veterans who learned to use ground, mass fire, and sheer persistence to overwhelm German defensive positions. By 1943 the Soviet rifle squad had evolved into one of the war's most tactically effective units, combining the DP's firepower with aggressive close-assault training and the willingness to accept casualties that no other army could match.
units.name: Rifle/MG

units.lore: Soviet submachine gun troops, armed primarily with the PPSh-41 or PPS-43, were among the most feared close-combat soldiers of the war. The PPSh's 71-round drum magazine and 900-round-per-minute cyclic rate gave a single squad the short-range firepower of a machine gun team, and entire battalions were sometimes equipped with nothing else for urban assault operations. At Stalingrad, Kursk, and Berlin, SMG companies led the assault through buildings, sewers, and trenches where rifles were too slow and the submachine gun's volume of fire was decisive; German survivors consistently reported that a Soviet SMG charge at close range was among the most terrifying experiences of the Eastern Front.
units.name: SMG

units.lore: The PTRD-41 anti-tank rifle was designed and produced in just two months following the German invasion — a remarkable engineering achievement that gave every Soviet rifle regiment an organic anti-armour weapon when proper anti-tank guns were desperately scarce. Its 14.5mm round could penetrate 25mm of armour at 300 meters, adequate for the Panzer I, II, and light armoured vehicles of 1941 but increasingly marginal as German armour thickened. By 1943 PTRD teams shifted to engaging vehicle optics, track links, and engine grilles rather than attempting frontal penetration, while also serving as a long-range anti-sniper and anti-personnel weapon against dug-in German positions.
units.name: PTRD Anti-Tank Rifle

units.lore: The Maxim M1910 water-cooled heavy machine gun on its distinctive wheeled Sokolov mount was the Soviet infantry's primary crew-served firepower from the Finnish War through Berlin — a weapon design from 1884 still effective sixty years later. Its 7.62mm belt-fed fire could sustain a beaten zone continuously for hours, and Soviet machine-gun sections were famous for their methodical fire discipline, registering fixed lines during daylight and delivering them precisely during night attacks. The Sokolov mount's small wheels allowed it to be rolled forward during an advance, giving it better tactical mobility than the tripod-mounted HMGs of most other armies.
units.name: Maksim HMG

units.lore: The Soviet 82mm mortar was the standard indirect fire weapon at battalion level, produced in enormous quantities and organic to every rifle battalion as a six-gun battery. Soviet doctrine placed great emphasis on mortar fire, recognizing that it was the weapon most likely to be available when artillery was not, and mortar platoons were trained to deliver rapid concentrations on German machine-gun nests and anti-tank gun positions just before the assault platoon closed. The 82mm's ability to be broken down and carried by the crew across terrain that defeated wheeled transport made it the Soviet Army's true artillery substitute in the close battles for villages, forests, and river crossings.
units.name: 82-BM-41 Mortar

units.lore: The Soviet 120mm heavy mortar was so effective that the Germans copied it directly after capturing examples in 1941, producing the Granatwerfer 42 from the same basic design. Its 16kg bomb could destroy a timber-roofed bunker with a direct hit and crater an anti-tank gun position from overhead — the high-angle fire reaching targets in dead ground that artillery couldn't reach. Soviet regimental mortar batteries with six 120mm weapons could neutralize a German company strongpoint in minutes, and the weapon's relatively simple construction allowed Soviet industry to produce them in vast quantities even during the catastrophic factory evacuations of 1941-42.
units.name: 120-PM-38 Mortar

units.lore: The Soviet 37mm automatic anti-aircraft gun was a direct development of the Swedish Bofors design, firing a 37mm round at 160 rounds per minute to engage aircraft up to 3,000 meters altitude. It equipped Soviet divisional anti-aircraft batteries throughout the war, though the Luftwaffe's early air superiority over the Eastern Front meant that these guns saw almost continuous combat action from 1941 through 1943. As Soviet air power grew after Kursk, the 37mm shifted increasingly to the ground-support role, its flat-trajectory rapid fire effective against infantry concentrations and soft-skin vehicles at ranges the 12.7mm DShK couldn't reach.
units.name: 37mm obr 1939 Gun

units.lore: The Soviet 85mm anti-aircraft gun was the Red Army's primary heavy AA weapon, capable of engaging bombers at 7,500 meters altitude with a shell that was also lethal to any German tank. Unlike the Germans, who enthusiastically used their 88mm FlaK in the anti-tank role, the Soviets were slower to employ the 85mm against armour — but when they did at Prokhorovka and in the anti-tank belts at Kursk, batteries of 85mm guns engaged Tiger tanks at ranges where the outcome was not in doubt. The success led directly to the adoption of the 85mm D-5T gun in the T-34/85 and KV-85, ending German heavy tank invulnerability.
units.name: 85mm obr 1939 Gun

units.lore: The 45mm anti-tank gun was the Red Army's standard battalion anti-tank weapon from 1941 onward, and its crews earned a desperate reputation for forward positioning — often pushing their guns to within 200-300 meters of approaching German tanks to achieve the close-range angles needed to penetrate thicker armour. The 1942 model with its improved ammunition could penetrate the Panzer III and IV at reasonable range but struggled with the Tiger and Panther even at close range. Soviet anti-tank crews suffered among the highest casualty rates of any branch, but their willingness to hold positions under direct tank fire and the sheer number of guns deployed frequently stopped German armoured breakthroughs that should have succeeded.
units.name: 45mm obr 1942 Gun

units.lore: The ZIS-2 57mm anti-tank gun was originally designed in 1941 but production was halted because its penetration performance exceeded all known German armour — making it apparently over-engineered for the cost. The appearance of the Tiger I and Panther in 1943 reversed this decision immediately, and production restarted at maximum rate. The ZIS-2's high-velocity shot could penetrate the Panther's side armour and threaten the Tiger I from the flanks at combat range, giving Soviet anti-tank regiments a weapon capable of handling the German heavy tanks that had previously been essentially invulnerable to towed guns below 85mm caliber.
units.name: 57mm ZIS-2 Gun

units.lore: The ZIS-3 76mm divisional gun was the most widely produced Soviet artillery piece of the war and arguably the finest all-purpose field gun on any side — light enough for the crew to manhandle, accurate enough for direct fire, and powerful enough in its anti-tank role to defeat most German armour at medium range until 1943. Stalin personally approved its design after initially rejecting it, and the brilliant engineer Vasily Grabin's factory switched to ZIS-3 production without waiting for official permission. At the Battle of Kursk, ZIS-3 batteries dug into the Soviet anti-tank belts destroyed more German tanks than any other weapon, firing over open sights at advancing Panzers.
units.name: 76mm ZIS-3 Gun

units.lore: The M-30 122mm howitzer was the standard Soviet divisional artillery piece, an outstanding design that remained in frontline service in some armies into the 21st century. Its 21.7kg shell could collapse a timber bunker, crater a road, or destroy a vehicle behind cover that flat-trajectory guns couldn't reach, and its 5,400-meter range let it reach deep into German defensive positions. Soviet artillery doctrine massed M-30s in concentrations of 200-300 guns per kilometer of breakthrough front, the density creating a rolling barrage that simply demolished everything in the assault lane — a technique perfected at Bagration in June 1944 that tore through German Army Group Centre in under a week.
units.name: 122mm obr 1938 Howitzer

units.lore: The D-1 152mm howitzer was designed specifically to be lighter and more mobile than its predecessors while still firing the powerful 40kg shell that made the 152mm class so effective against German fortifications. Its breakthrough-fire capability — destroying concrete bunkers, demolishing buildings, and neutralizing dug-in 88mm positions — made it the weapon of choice for the methodical Soviet assault gun preparations before major offensive operations. Batteries of D-1s fired in the counter-battery role against German medium artillery before the main assault, then shifted to direct support as the attack went in, their heavy shells capable of defeating the field fortifications that 122mm fire could only suppress.
units.name: 152mm obr 1943 Howitzer

units.lore: The T-70 was an attempt to replace the T-60 light tank with a vehicle that could carry a two-man crew and a more useful 45mm gun, while remaining simple enough to be produced in automotive rather than tank factories. Though outclassed by German medium tanks in any direct engagement, T-70s were used in large numbers throughout 1942-43 for cavalry-style exploitation, flank screening, and infantry support in terrain where heavier tanks couldn't follow. At Prokhorovka, T-70s fought alongside T-34s in desperate close-range melee against Tiger I and Panzer IV tanks, their 45mm guns ineffective against German frontal armour but their numbers contributing to the chaos that eventually halted the German advance.
units.name: T-70 obr 1942/43

units.lore: The T-34 of 1941 shocked German tank crews and commanders when they first encountered it — its sloped armour deflected rounds from the standard 37mm and short 50mm anti-tank guns, while its 76mm F-34 gun could penetrate most German armour at combat range. The early model suffered from a cramped two-man turret, poor visibility, no turret basket, and unreliable transmission, but these mechanical flaws mattered less than its fundamental soundness: the T-34 was faster, better-armoured, and better-gunned than the Panzer III it most commonly fought. German tank tactics and superior crew training overcame the technical surprise in 1941-42, but the T-34 established the template that Soviet tank design would refine throughout the war.
units.name: T-34 obr 1941/42
units.armament: 76mm F-34 gun, Co-ax MG, Hull MG

units.lore: The T-34/85 was the Soviet response to the Tiger and Panther — a significantly enlarged three-man turret housing the 85mm D-5T gun that could penetrate Panther frontal armour at 500 meters and Tiger I at even shorter range. It entered service in early 1944 and quickly became the standard Soviet medium tank, manufactured in numbers that dwarfed German production: over 35,000 were built by the war's end. At Bagration, Vistula-Oder, and the Berlin operation, T-34/85s spearheaded the great Soviet offensives that destroyed entire German army groups, their superior numbers and adequate firepower achieving through mass what individual German tanks could not match through quality.
units.name: T-34/85 obr 1943
units.armament: 85mm D-5T gun, Co-ax MG, Hull MG

units.lore: The KV-1s (skorostniy — speedy) was an attempt to address the KV-1's catastrophic speed and reliability problems by reducing armour thickness and fitting a smaller, lighter turret. The result was still slower than the T-34 and carried only the same 76mm gun, raising questions about whether any improvement over the T-34 justified the KV's greater complexity and resource cost. The KV-1s saw heavy use at Kursk and in the summer 1943 operations, but its replacement by the KV-85 — and eventually by the IS-2 — acknowledged that the heavy tank requirement needed a genuinely heavier gun rather than a marginally faster chassis.
units.name: KV-1s
units.armament: 76mm F-34 gun, Co-ax MG, Hull MG

units.lore: The KV-85 was a stopgap measure in 1943, mounting the new 85mm D-5T gun in a new, larger turret on the KV-1s chassis to give the Red Army a heavy tank that could engage Tigers and Panthers on better terms while the IS-2 completed development. Only 148 were produced before IS-2 production began in earnest, but these saw action at Leningrad and in the battles following Kursk. Crews appreciated the powerful gun but were aware that the KV hull's armour — though thick — was vertical rather than sloped, making it less effective than its nominal thickness suggested against the long 88mm of the Tiger I.
units.name: KV-85

units.lore: The IS-2 (Iosif Stalin-2) was the Soviet Union's definitive answer to German heavy armour — a tank with 120mm of sloped frontal armour and a 122mm D-25T gun whose 25kg shell could penetrate Tiger I and Panther armour and physically destroy the turret of any German tank through a direct hit. Its slow reload time (two to three rounds per minute due to separate ammunition) was a tactical liability in fast-moving tank battles, but Soviet doctrine used IS-2s as breakthrough weapons rather than tank destroyers — leading assaults through prepared defenses, defeating fortifications, and suppressing heavy anti-tank guns while T-34s handled mobile combat.
units.name: IS-2

units.lore: The OT-34 (Ognemyotny Tank — flamethrower tank) replaced the T-34's hull machine gun with the ATO-42 flame projector, concealing the modification so that German crews couldn't identify flame tanks from battle tanks until they opened fire. Soviet doctrine employed OT-34s in special flamethrower tank battalions attached to rifle corps for assault operations against German prepared positions, where the armoured protection of the T-34 hull allowed them to close to the 60-100 meter range needed for the flame projector. German defenders who held against conventional tanks consistently broke when OT-34s appeared, the combination of armour and fire being psychologically overwhelming.
units.name: OT-34

units.lore: The KV-8s combined the armoured protection of the KV-1s with an ATO-42 flamethrower replacing the main gun, the 45mm stub gun in the turret being fitted to make the flame tank less identifiable as a specialist vehicle. The KV chassis gave the flamethrower operator 90mm of frontal armour — far more than the OT-34 — allowing it to survive at the very close ranges where flamethrowers are effective even under intensive defensive fire. Assigned to heavy flamethrower tank units, KV-8s were used in the assault on the Dnieper fortifications and in the fighting through the Baltic states in 1944.
units.name: KV-8s
units.armament: 45mm obr 1938 gun, ATO-42 FT, Hull MG

units.lore: The SU-76M was the most produced Soviet armoured fighting vehicle after the T-34, with over 14,000 built, and it served as the primary infantry support weapon for rifle divisions that had no organic armoured assets. Its open-topped superstructure was notorious — crews called it the "Bitch" and the "Glass Coffin" for its vulnerability to shell fragments — but its ZIS-3 76mm gun provided direct fire support against German machine-gun nests, bunkers, and anti-tank guns at the distances infantry operated. Soviet doctrine paired SU-76 batteries with rifle battalions for the final assault, their mobility allowing them to follow infantry through terrain that towed guns couldn't negotiate.
units.name: SU-76M

units.lore: The SU-85 was rushed into production in mid-1943 specifically to counter the Tiger I and Panther after the T-34's 76mm gun proved unable to reliably penetrate their frontal armour at battle ranges. Mounting the 85mm D-5S in the SU-122 chassis, it gave Soviet armoured formations a dedicated tank destroyer capable of engaging German heavy armour at 1,000-1,500 meters — the engagement ranges at which Tigers preferred to fight. SU-85 regiments were attached to tank corps for operations, advancing with the tanks and engaging long-range targets while T-34s handled close-range fighting; the type became obsolete once the T-34/85 entered service, but remained in use until the war's end.
units.name: SU-85

units.lore: The SU-122 mounted the M-30 122mm howitzer on the T-34 chassis to create a powerful close-support weapon capable of demolishing fortifications and neutralizing anti-tank gun positions that the T-34's direct fire couldn't destroy. Its 21.7kg HE shell could devastate a building, collapse a trench, or destroy a light vehicle with a near miss, while its HEAT round could penetrate any German tank hull at close range — a function Soviet crews rarely relied on as the howitzer's slow reload was a liability in fast-moving tank combat. Used primarily in the direct-fire infantry support role, SU-122s gave Soviet combined-arms attacks an armoured battering ram for fixed defenses.
units.name: SU-122

units.lore: The SU-152 earned the nickname "Zveroboy" (Beast-Killer) at Kursk in July 1943, where its 152mm ML-20 gun demolished Tiger I and Elephant tank destroyers with a single HE round — not by penetrating their armour, but by physically tearing off turrets and bending hulls with the concussive force of a 43.5kg shell at close range. Designed as a heavy assault gun for fortified positions, it proved unexpectedly effective as a tank destroyer simply because no German armour could survive a direct hit from a weapon in its class. SU-152s served in independent heavy self-propelled artillery regiments attached to tank armies for breakthrough operations.
units.name: SU-152

units.lore: The ISU-122 mounted the 122mm D-25S gun — identical to the IS-2's armament — on the IS chassis, creating a dedicated tank destroyer with the firepower to defeat any German vehicle and the armour to survive defensive fire from most German guns. Soviet doctrine used ISU-122 regiments in the anti-armour role, deploying them in hull-down positions to engage German tanks at long range while IS-2s and T-34s conducted the assault. Unlike the earlier SU-122, the ISU-122's high-velocity gun was designed primarily as an anti-armour weapon rather than a howitzer, giving it flat-trajectory accuracy against specific targets at ranges over 1,500 meters.
units.name: ISU-122

units.lore: The ISU-152 was the successor to the SU-152, mounting the same 152mm ML-20 howitzer on the superior IS chassis to give it dramatically better armour protection — 90mm of sloped frontal plate versus the SU-152's 60mm — while retaining the devastating firepower that had earned the "Beast-Killer" reputation. ISU-152 regiments spearheaded the assault on every major German fortified city in the final campaigns: Königsberg, Breslau, Budapest, Vienna, and Berlin, where their heavy shells could demolish a building occupied by German infantry without exposing assault troops to the direct fire that defended street corners and windows.
units.name: ISU-152

units.lore: The BM-13 Katyusha rocket launcher — known to Soviet troops as "Katyusha" and to German soldiers as "Stalin's Organ" for its distinctive wailing howl — became one of the most iconic weapons of the Eastern Front, mounted on Lend-Lease Studebaker trucks and capable of ripple-firing sixteen 132mm rockets in under ten seconds. Its accuracy was poor by conventional artillery standards, but massed Katyusha salvos from entire Guards Mortar regiments created saturation fire effects over large areas that no human target could survive. The Katyusha's psychological effect was immense: the screaming rockets, the sudden total destruction of a target area, and the inability of defenders to identify where the next salvo would land broke unit cohesion as effectively as casualties.
units.name: BM-13 Katyusha

units.lore: The DShK 12.7mm heavy machine gun mounted on truck or halftrack gave Soviet units mobile anti-aircraft defence against the Luftwaffe ground-attack aircraft that plagued Soviet columns throughout 1941-43. Its heavy round could engage aircraft up to 1,500 meters altitude and penetrate the cockpit armour of most German attack aircraft, making it a meaningful deterrent. DShK-equipped trucks were organic to Soviet armoured and motorised units, positioned at column intervals to create overlapping fields of fire during road movement; they also proved highly effective as ground-fire weapons against infantry, light vehicles, and crew-served weapons when the air threat was absent.
units.name: DShK AA MG on Truck

units.lore: The M17 Multiple Gun Motor Carriage arrived via Lend-Lease with its quad .50-caliber Browning M45 Maxson turret, and Soviet tank corps used it as a mobile close-range anti-aircraft weapon riding alongside T-34 columns. The four .50-caliber guns produced an overwhelming volume of fire against low-flying German attack aircraft and dive bombers, while the M3 halftrack chassis gave it the cross-country mobility to keep up with armoured units on the move. By 1944, as Soviet air power grew and the Luftwaffe's ground-attack threat diminished, Soviet M17 crews focused primarily on the ground support role — the quadruple .50-caliber fire being devastating against infantry in the open.
units.name: ZSU M17 MGMC

units.lore: The BA-64 was the Soviet Union's primary light armoured car from 1942 onward, a small two-man vehicle intended for reconnaissance, liaison, and flank screening in the motorised cavalry and mechanised roles. Its light armour protected only against small-arms fire, and the open-topped turret mounting a DT machine gun (or PTRD anti-tank rifle in some variants) gave it minimal combat capability against any serious opposition. Soviet reconnaissance doctrine accepted these limitations, using BA-64s in pairs to probe roads and identify German outpost lines while heavier vehicles remained out of contact, their speed and small profile making them difficult targets for anti-tank gun teams.
units.name: BA-64

units.lore: The Ford GPA "Seep" (Sea Jeep) was an amphibious version of the Willys Jeep supplied to the Soviet Union under Lend-Lease, used primarily for river crossings and reconnaissance of Soviet waterway obstacles that were ubiquitous on the Eastern Front. Soviet tank armies facing multiple river lines — the Don, Dnieper, Vistula, Oder — used amphibious vehicles to push reconnaissance teams across before engineer bridges were completed, allowing commanders to assess German defensive positions on the far bank before committing main forces to the crossing. The GPA's water performance was modest but adequate for calm rivers, and its cross-country ability on land matched the standard Willys Jeep.
units.name: Ford GPA Amphibious Jeep

units.lore: The ZIS-5 3-ton truck was the Soviet Army's standard cargo vehicle from the 1930s onward, supplemented from 1942 by thousands of American Dodge 3/4-ton and Studebaker 6x6 trucks under Lend-Lease — vehicles Soviet commanders praised as superior to their domestic equivalents. Motorised rifle brigades used these trucks to follow tank columns and deploy infantry for the assault on objectives that armour alone could not hold, while logistics units relied on truck columns to push ammunition and fuel forward at the pace needed for deep operations. Soviet commanders credited the massive Lend-Lease truck shipment — over 375,000 vehicles — as essential to the mobility that made operations like Bagration possible.
units.name: ZIS-5/6/Dodge Truck

units.lore: Soviet rifle divisions in 1941-43 relied heavily on horse-drawn transport for artillery, supplies, and wounded because motorized vehicles were desperately scarce. The tachanka — a horse-drawn cart with a Maxim machine gun — had been a signature weapon of the Civil War, and horse cavalry remained a significant force through 1942 when mechanised substitutes were unavailable. Even as the Red Army motorized in 1943-44, horse-drawn wagons continued moving ammunition forward on the final leg to the firing line where trucks couldn't follow due to terrain or enemy fire, and field kitchens, medical units, and engineer bridging equipment often relied on horses throughout the war.
units.name: Horse-Drawn Wagon

units.lore: The Stalinets S-65 was a Soviet tracked agricultural tractor converted to military use as an artillery prime mover, a common improvisation in an army chronically short of purpose-built military transport. Its powerful diesel engine could haul the heaviest Soviet field guns — 122mm howitzers, 152mm guns, even 203mm howitzers — across the muddy terrain and snow that defeated wheeled vehicles, making it invaluable during the rasputitsa seasons when the Eastern Front ground to a near halt. Slow and mechanically demanding, Stalinets tractors moved at walking pace and consumed enormous quantities of fuel, but in the absence of better options they kept Soviet heavy artillery mobile through the most difficult terrain of the war.
units.name: Stalinets
